name: Beauregard
image_alt: Large orange-fleshed sweet potatoes with rose-purple skin
description: The most widely grown sweet potato in the United States — rose-purple skin with deep orange, intensely sweet flesh. Consistently high yields, excellent disease resistance, and reliable performance across a wide range of climates make it the benchmark variety.
history: Developed at Louisiana State University and released in 1987. Quickly became the commercial standard for sweet potato production in the American South. Named for the Louisiana parish where LSU's Sweet Potato Research Station is located.
seasonal_quirks: Sweet potatoes need a long, hot growing season — plant slips only after soil is thoroughly warm (65°F+). Beauregard is among the more adaptable varieties and produces well further north than most. Vines spread aggressively and can be trained to keep beds tidy.
harvest_cues: Harvest before first frost — cold damages tubers in the ground. Leaves yellowing and vines dying back signal readiness. Dig carefully with a fork, working well away from the crown. Cure immediately at 85°F and high humidity for 10–14 days — this is essential for flavor and storage.
variety_notes: The curing process converts starches to sugars — uncured Beauregard is starchy and bland, properly cured it is intensely sweet. One of the few sweet potato varieties widely available as slips from garden centers.
preservation: Cure at 85°F and 85–90% humidity for 10–14 days. Store at 55–60°F — not the refrigerator. Properly cured and stored sweet potatoes keep 6–12 months.
